chunks.text: Sheila Morrin Humphreys was the first EECS Director of Diversity from 1982-2015. In the late 1970s, EECS graduate students Paula Hawthorn and Barbara Simons approached Professor Margaret Wilkerson to propose workshops exposing women to engineering and computer science. Wilkerson asked associate director Sheila Humphreys to work with STEM students to promote opportunities for women in these fields. This began a 40-year odyssey where Humphreys applied her energy to helping minority groups pursue untraditional careers. She nurtured generations of students to achieve their educational goals in STEM fields and was instrumental in creating programs for diversity, equity, and inclusion at Berkeley.

Humphreys graduated from Smith College in 1963 and earned a Ph.D. in Francophone African literature from Tufts University in 1974. She moved to the west coast in 1975, working as assistant dean of admissions at Mills College. After learning from Lenore Blum that mathematics was a critical filter for women in STEM, she recruited math and science-oriented female high school students to Mills and encouraged them to enroll in Blum's fast-track pre-calculus program. She was recruited to Berkeley in 1977 to work for the Center for the Continuing Education of Women (CCEW).

Humphreys joined the EECS department in 1982, hired by Dean Karl Pister as the first academic director of graduate outreach in Berkeley Engineering. She served as Director of the EECS Center for Undergraduate Matters and Student Diversity. Her collaboration with Hawthorn and Simons led to the Computer Science Reentry Program (1982-1996), which provided an alternative path to graduate school for underrepresented minorities in computer science. The program produced ten Ph.D. and 39 M.S. degrees, with notable alumnae including Diane Greene, founder of VMware, and Nina Amenta, professor at UC Davis.

Another legacy was the creation of the Excellence and Diversity Student Programs in 1986 with Chair Eugene Wong and Vice-Chair Michael Lieberman. This included a program that tripled minority graduate student enrollment. She collaborated with Black graduate students to create the Summer Undergraduate Program in Engineering Research at Berkeley (SUPERB), which prepares undergraduates for graduate degrees in STEM. 76% of the first SUPERB cohort changed their minds about graduate school, and their admission rate to Berkeley EECS was three times that of non-SUPERB students. SUPERB has remained active for 30 years.

Humphreys tackled systemic inequity through various roles: member of the NSF Empowering Leadership Alliance, invited member of a CRA working group on minority graduate student recruitment, participant in the Berkeley Edge Program, Berkeley representative to the National Coalition for Women in Computing, and member of the Berkeley Coalition for Excellence in Mathematics, Science, and Engineering. Since retiring in 2015, she remains active, working with the Berkeley Engineering and Science Alumni Club (BESAC), mentoring in the Cal NERDS program, and co-chairing the 150W History Project for 150 Years of Women at Berkeley.

Humphreys' impact has been recognized with several awards: UC Berkeley Distinguished Service Team Award (1999), CRA A. Nico Habermann Award (1999), WEPAN Women in Engineering Program Award (2002), UC Berkeley Chancellor's Outstanding Staff Award (2011), Presidential Award for Excellence in Science, Mathematics and Engineering Mentoring (2012) from President Obama, Berkeley Citation (2013), and Outstanding Advisor in Cal NERDS (2015). She continues to work on a monograph about diversity history in Berkeley Engineering and contributes to campus initiatives.
